---
title: "4.1.4 Interrupts"
subtitle: "Software P161-P164"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .figure-placeholder { border: 1px dashed #8aa0bf; border-radius: 8px; background: #f4f8ff; color: #40526b; padding: 1.1em 1.3em; margin: 0.8em 0; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.74em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.34em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 4 Directory / 返回 Unit 4 目录](../../index.html)

## Interrupts

### Signal to the microprocessor

An <span class="term">interrupt</span> (中断) is a signal sent from a device or from software to the microprocessor.

This causes the microprocessor to <span class="mark">temporarily stop</span> what it is doing so that it can service the interrupt.

## Causes of Interrupts

### Signals, processes and faults

Interrupts can be caused by:

::: {.tight-list}
- a timing signal
- an input/output process, such as a disk drive or printer requiring more data
- a hardware fault, such as a paper jam in the printer
:::

## Causes of Interrupts

### User and software events

Interrupts can also be caused by:

::: {.tight-list}
- user interaction, such as pressing keys on a keyboard
- software errors that cause a problem
- an `.exe` file that cannot be found
- two processes trying to access the same memory location
- an attempt to divide by zero
:::

## Servicing an Interrupt

### Identify type and priority

Once the interrupt signal is received, the microprocessor either:

::: {.tight-list}
- carries on with what it was doing
- or stops to service the device or program that caused the interrupt
:::

The computer needs to identify the interrupt type and establish the <span class="term">interrupt priority</span> (中断优先级).

## Interrupts and Multitasking

### Co-existing tasks

Interrupts allow computers to carry out many tasks or have several windows open at the same time.

For example, a user may download a file from the internet while listening to music from a library.

The user has the impression that both functions are being carried out simultaneously, but data is being passed in and out of memory very rapidly.

## Buffers

### Temporary memory area

A <span class="term">buffer</span> (缓冲区) is a memory area that stores data temporarily.

Buffers can compensate for different speeds, for example:

::: {.tight-list}
- downloading a movie from the internet
- sending data to a media player
- sending data to a printer
:::

::: {.figure-placeholder}
Figure 4.12 Use of a buffer when sending data to a printer screenshot placeholder
:::

## Buffers and Streaming

### Avoiding freezing

When streaming a movie, the data transmission rate from the web server to the buffer must be greater than the rate at which data is transmitted from buffer to media player.

Without buffers, the movie would frequently <span class="mark">freeze</span>.

## Interrupt Service Routine

### Saving and restoring the current task

Whenever an interrupt is received it needs to be serviced.

The sequence includes:

::: {.tight-list}
- the status of the current task is saved
- the contents of the <span class="term">Program Counter (PC)</span> and other registers are saved
- the <span class="term">interrupt service routine (ISR)</span> is executed
- the status of the interrupted task is reinstated after servicing
:::

## Buffers and Interrupts

### Why they are used together

Buffers and interrupts are often used together to allow standard computer functions to be carried out.

Without buffers and interrupts, the microprocessor could remain idle waiting for a document to be printed.

This would waste microprocessor time. Buffers and interrupts allow the microprocessor to carry on with other tasks while printing continues.

## Printer Example

### Figure 4.13

Figure 4.13 shows how buffers and interrupts are used when a document is sent from memory to a printer.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
Figure 4.13 Use of interrupts and buffers when printing a document screenshot placeholder
:::

## Activity 4.1

### Classroom practice prompts

Use the textbook activity to check understanding:

::: {.tight-list}
- identify whether named software is system software or application software
- explain how a buffer can stop a video constantly freezing
- describe how interrupts can allow a video to play while a document prints
- describe what happens if a printer runs out of black ink
:::

## Classroom Check

### Explain interrupts and buffers

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- an interrupt is a signal from a device or software to a microprocessor
- the microprocessor temporarily stops to service the interrupt
- the interrupt type and priority must be identified
- a buffer stores data temporarily
- the PC and registers are saved before the ISR runs
- buffers and interrupts prevent the CPU from waiting idly for slow devices
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Programming languages and translators

The next deck starts Section 4.2 with high-level and low-level languages.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 4 Directory / 返回 Unit 4 目录](../../index.html)
